title: Aglaia angustifolia
what_description: Aglaia angustifolia is a species of tree in the family Meliaceae, native to Southeast Asia. It is a small to medium-sized tree, growing up to 15 meters tall, with a straight trunk and smooth, grayish-brown bark. The leaves are simple, alternate, and elliptical in shape, measuring 4-10 cm in length and 2-5 cm in width, with a dark green color and smooth margin. The flowers are small, yellowish-green, and fragrant, arranged in axillary panicles, while the fruit is a three-winged capsule, containing 3-5 seeds, each measuring 2-3 cm in length. The wood of Aglaia angustifolia is dense and hard, with a specific gravity of 0.85-0.95, and is used for furniture and construction purposes.
ailments_description: The most common ailments you can heal with Aglaia angustifolia are diarrhea and fever, which are often treated with extracts of its leaves and roots, while inflammation is reduced through its anti-inflammatory properties. Rheumatism sufferers have seen improvement due to its anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties, and skin infections are cured with its antimicrobial activity. Malaria treatment has also been effective using its extracts, as well as gastroenteritis and dysentery, which are treated with its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. Cough and asthma are relieved with its expectorant properties, and fungal infections are cured with its antifungal properties. Furthermore, wound healing is accelerated, hypertension is reduced, pain is alleviated, infection is prevented, diabetes is managed, inflammatory diseases are treated, arthritis is relieved, and dyspepsia is cured.
properties_description: The main therapeutic properties of Aglaia angustifolia are attributed to its ability to exhibit antioxidant activity, which is primarily due to the presence of flavonoids such as kaempferol and quercetin, that neutralize free radicals and protect against oxidative stress. Additionally, Aglaia angustifolia has been found to possess antimicrobial properties, which are attributed to the bioactive compounds saponins and triterpenoids, that inhibit the growth of various microorganisms, including bacteria and fungi. The plant's anti-inflammatory properties are thought to be mediated by the action of volatile oils, which reduce inflammation and alleviate pain, while its analgesic properties are attributed to the presence of alkaloids and glycosides, which interact with opioid receptors to produce analgesia. Furthermore, Aglaia angustifolia has been found to exhibit antifungal activity, which is attributed to the action of saponins and other bioactive compounds that inhibit the growth of fungi and prevent the formation of fungal biofilms. The bioactive compounds present in Aglaia angustifolia work in synergy to produce its medicinal properties, making it a valuable plant for the development of new therapeutic agents.
constituents_description: The most important healing constituents of Aglaia angustifolia are aglaianone, aglaiaflavone, aglatinin A, aglatinin B, and quercetin. These compounds have been isolated and characterized from various parts of the plant and exhibit significant bioactivity. Aglaianone and aglaiaflavone have been reported to possess anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, while aglatinin A and B have shown antimicrobial activity against certain bacteria and fungi. Quercetin, a well-known flavonoid, has been found to have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, as well as potential anti-cancer effects. The presence of these bioactive compounds in Aglaia angustifolia may contribute to its traditional uses in medicine, including treating fever, rheumatism, and other inflammatory conditions.
preparations_description: The main herbal preparations of Aglaia angustifolia are infusion, tincture, tea, decoction and salve. Infusion is a preparation made by steeping the leaves of Aglaia angustifolia in hot water to extract its bioactive compounds, often used for its anti-inflammatory properties. Tincture is a concentrated liquid preparation made by soaking the leaves in a solvent such as ethanol or glycerin to create a standardized extract, commonly used for its antimicrobial effects. Tea is a preparation made by steeping the leaves in hot water and is often used for its antioxidant properties, decoction is a preparation made by boiling the roots or bark in water to extract its bioactive compounds, and salve is a topical preparation made by infusing the leaves in a carrier oil to create a therapeutic cream, often used for its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial effects. These preparations are typically used to treat a range of health conditions including fever, cough, and skin infections due to the plant's broad spectrum of bioactive compounds.
side_effects_description: The possible side effects of using Aglaia angustifolia improperly are induce nausea due to its toxic compounds, particularly aglacones, which can irritate the stomach lining and cause stomach upset. Additionally, it can cause diarrhea and trigger vomiting as the body attempts to eliminate the toxins. Furthermore, improper use of Aglaia angustifolia can lead to allergic reactions, ranging from mild skin irritation to severe anaphylaxis, due to the presence of allergenic compounds such as limonoids. This is because the plant's bioactive compounds can interact with the body's immune system, causing an overreaction and triggering an allergic response. It is essential to use this plant under the guidance of a qualified medical professional to minimize the risk of these side effects.
uses: Aglaia angustifolia is traditionally used in folk medicine in Southeast Asia for its antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and antioxidant properties. Its leaves are used to treat various ailments, including fever, rheumatism, and skin conditions. The plant's extracts have been shown to have potential in inhibiting the growth of certain bacteria and fungi. Additionally, Aglaia angustifolia is used to treat digestive issues and as a remedy for snake bites in some traditional practices. Further research is needed to confirm the full scope of its medicinal uses and benefits.
taxonomy: Aglaia angustifolia is classified as a species of plant in the family Meliaceae. This family includes a variety of trees and shrubs commonly known as the mahogany family. Aglaia angustifolia is a member of the genus Aglaia, which is native to tropical and subtropical regions of Asia and the Pacific. The genus Aglaia consists of approximately 100 species of trees and shrubs. These plants are often found in forests and woodlands.
benefits: Aglaia angustifolia has been found to possess various health benefits, including antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, which may help in reducing oxidative stress and inflammation in the body. The plant is also known to exhibit antimicrobial and antifungal activities, making it a potential candidate for treating infections and other microbial-related diseases. Additionally, its extracts have been reported to have cardiovascular and neuroprotective effects, which could contribute to the prevention of heart disease and neurodegenerative disorders. Furthermore, Aglaia angustifolia is said to have anti-diabetic properties, which may aid in managing blood sugar levels and insulin sensitivity. More research is needed to fully understand the potential health benefits of Aglaia angustifolia and to confirm its efficacy as a medicinal plant.
parts: Aglaia angustifolia is a plant species with reported medicinal properties, but the specific details regarding its medicinal parts are limited in available information.
